message:
Improve core modules and expand benchmarks

- Refactor benchmarks with common utilities
- Improve error handling throughout core
- Enhance config, ingest, IPC, and storage modules
- Improve output formatters and renderers
- Update daemon integration tests
- Update AGENTS.md and beads tracking

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"wa-4vx.10.17","title":"E2E script: pane exclude filter prevents capture (ignored pane absent from search, shown in status)","description":"# Task: E2E script — pane exclude filter

## Goal
Validate that pane selection filters protect privacy and behave predictably:
- excluded panes are visible (as ignored) but not ingested
- excluded panes do not appear in search results

## Scenario
- Spawn two dummy panes:
  1) "observed" pane prints OBSERVED_TOKEN
  2) "ignored" pane prints SECRET_TOKEN and has a title/cwd that matches an exclude rule

- Configure wa with an exclude rule that matches the ignored pane.

Steps
1) Start wa watch with the configured workspace.
2) Wait until the observed token is persisted (via wa robot search).
3) Assert that searching for SECRET_TOKEN returns no results.
4) Assert wa status / wa robot state shows the ignored pane as ignored with a reason.

## Assertions
- Observed pane is captured and searchable.
- Ignored pane is not captured and not searchable.
- Status output makes the ignore decision obvious.

## Artifacts
- Effective config used (including pane rules).
- wa watch logs with decision messages (no secrets).
- wa robot state JSON.
- wa robot search JSON for both tokens.

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`), including prerequisites and default inclusion status.
- Ensure `./scripts/e2e_test.sh --all` includes this case (or mark non-default with justification).

## Logging
- Follow the standard E2E harness logging contract (`wa-4vx.10.6`) and runner (`wa-4vx.10.11`).
- Emit per-step start/end markers with timestamps + durations.
- Include case name and pane ids in every log line.
- Redact secrets (device codes, tokens) in logs and artifacts.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Test is deterministic.
- On failure, artifacts show whether the bug is:
  - filter matching
  - ingest mistakenly tailing ignored panes
  - search indexing
  - status/state reporting


## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - Assert that SECRET_TOKEN never appears in artifacts/logs (privacy guarantee), not just “not searchable”.
  - Add a control assertion that OBSERVED_TOKEN does appear in search to avoid false negatives.

- Artifact validation:
  - Assert state/status artifacts include the ignore reason so the user can understand why capture was skipped.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.10.18","title":"E2E script: secret redaction in audit/export (no raw secrets in artifacts)","description":"# Task: E2E script — secret redaction in audit/export

## Goal
Prove that end-to-end audit and export paths never leak secrets from action inputs.

This is a trust-critical property:
- users will paste artifacts into issues
- CI will upload artifacts
- we must ensure secrets are not present in these outputs

## Scenario
- Spawn a dummy pane that echoes received input.
- Ensure policy allows a send in a safe context.

Steps
1) Send a string containing a known fake secret pattern (e.g., `sk-FAKESECRET123`) via `wa send` or `wa robot send`.
2) Export audit trail slice for this run (or query audit feed).
3) Assert that:
  - the audit entry exists
  - the raw secret string does NOT appear anywhere in audit export output
  - a redacted placeholder DOES appear (to prove redaction happened, not omission)

## Assertions
- Audit emission records the action.
- Redaction rules remove the secret from:
  - audit DB row(s)
  - audit export output
  - E2E script logs + artifacts

## Artifacts
- Effective config used.
- Audit export JSONL.
- Script stdout/stderr.
- Grep-style proof output (counts of secret matches should be 0).

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`), including prerequisites and default inclusion status.
- Ensure `./scripts/e2e_test.sh --all` includes this case (or mark non-default with justification).

## Acceptance Criteria
- The test is deterministic.
- Any failure clearly indicates whether the leak came from:
  - audit emission
  - redaction engine
  - export path
  - e2e script logging


## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - The script must scan *all* produced artifacts (logs, JSONL exports, summaries) for the fake secret string.
  - Include at least two different fake secret patterns (OpenAI-style, GitHub token-style) to ensure redactor coverage isn’t overly narrow.

- Failure mode:
  - If a match is found, print the exact artifact filename + surrounding context (bounded) to make the leak diagnosable.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.10.19","title":"E2E script: workspace isolation (no cross-project DB leakage; --workspace honored)","description":"# Task: E2E script — workspace isolation

## Goal
Prove that wa honors workspace scoping and does not leak data across projects.

This is a critical UX property:
- users run multiple projects in parallel
- agents should not read/write the wrong DB
- debugging depends on knowing which workspace you are looking at

## Scenario
- Spawn a dummy pane that prints a unique token: WORKSPACE_TOKEN.

Steps
1) Start wa watch with --workspace <A>.
2) Wait until WORKSPACE_TOKEN is persisted (wa robot search --workspace <A>).
3) Run wa robot search --workspace <B> for WORKSPACE_TOKEN.

Assertions
- In workspace A: the token is found.
- In workspace B: the token is NOT found.

Optional extension
- Start wa watch in workspace B and ensure it creates a distinct DB.

## Artifacts
- effective config for both workspaces
- wa watch logs
- wa robot search outputs for A and B

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`), including prerequisites and default inclusion status.
- Ensure `./scripts/e2e_test.sh --all` includes this case (or mark non-default with justification).

## Logging
- Follow the standard E2E harness logging contract (`wa-4vx.10.6`) and runner (`wa-4vx.10.11`).
- Emit per-step start/end markers with timestamps + durations.
- Include case name and pane ids in every log line.
- Redact secrets (device codes, tokens) in logs and artifacts.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Test is deterministic.
- Failures make it obvious whether the bug is:
  - workspace resolution
  - DB path derivation
  - CLI flag propagation


## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - Assert the two workspaces produce distinct DB/log paths (not just search behavior).
  - Add a control assertion that workspace B can find a different token once it has ingested something.

- Artifact validation:
  - Artifacts must include the derived paths (workspace root, db path, log path) to make mis-scoping diagnosable.

-{"id":"wa-4vx.10.2","title":"Criterion benchmarks + budgets (pattern detect, delta extraction, FTS query)","description":"# Task: Benchmarks + budgets

## Goal
Prevent performance regressions on hot paths.

This is the “performance test suite” complement to unit/E2E tests: it ensures wa stays fast enough to run continuously.

## Bench targets (must exist)
- Overlap/delta extraction (hot ingest path)
- Pattern engine quick reject (typical no-match)
- Pattern detection on “typical pane output”
- FTS query latency for common queries on representative DB size
- Watcher loop overhead when idle (per-pane check cost)

## Initial budgets (PLAN §13.4 + Appendix G.7)
These are v0 targets; we can tune later, but they must be explicitly recorded so regressions are real failures, not vibes.

- Quick reject no-match: **< 1µs** for typical non-matching text.
- Pattern detection (typical corpus): **p50 < 1ms**, **p99 < 5ms**.
- FTS query common patterns (DB ~100k captures):
  - **p50 < 10ms**, **p99 < 50ms** (hard cap: < 50ms for common queries).
- Watcher loop overhead (idle): **< 100µs per pane check**.

Notes:
- Budgets should be reported with machine metadata (OS/CPU/rustc/features).
- CI should enforce budgets in a non-flaky way (relative threshold vs baseline, or dedicated perf runners).

## CI integration
- Local-only initially is acceptable if CI is too noisy, but the harness must produce machine-readable artifacts.
- Once stable, integrate into CI (tracked by `wa-nu4.3.4.4`) with:
  - clear pass/fail thresholds
  - artifact upload of benchmark results on failure

## Artifacts & logging
Bench runs must produce debuggable artifacts:
- raw Criterion JSON output
- a short summary (top regressions, top slowest benches)
- build/machine metadata:
  - OS, CPU model, rustc version, enabled features
  - git commit hash when available

## Testing
- Bench harness self-check:
  - Add a small “sanity bench” guaranteed to run so the harness failing to execute is caught early.

- Budget enforcement tests:
  - If budget checks are implemented as code, include a unit test that feeds a fake benchmark JSON and asserts:
    - regression triggers failure
    - outputs include which benchmark regressed and by how much

## Acceptance Criteria
- Bench suite runs locally and produces a baseline.
- Budgets above are documented and emitted in summaries.
- CI enforcement can come later if early CI is too noisy, but the artifacts/logging are in place.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.10.20","title":"E2E suite completeness: runner --all covers all cases + stable case registry","description":"# Task: E2E suite completeness (case registry)

## Goal
Make sure `./scripts/e2e_test.sh --all` is a comprehensive, maintained suite that:
- runs every **registered** E2E scenario (not just the ones under `wa-4vx.10.*`)
- produces stable artifacts per case
- prints a clear PASS/FAIL summary

This task exists to avoid coupling “runner quality” to “suite completeness”.

## Requirements
- Maintain a single authoritative registry of cases (names + descriptions).
- The registry must also record (at least):
  - prerequisites (e.g., requires WezTerm, requires docker, requires feature flags)
  - whether the case is part of the default `--all` run
  - a human-readable “why this case exists” note (what it protects)

- `./scripts/e2e_test.sh --all` iterates that registry.
- Each case has:
  - deterministic steps
  - explicit assertions
  - artifacts directory
  - clear failure summary

## Cross-repo case coverage (important)
Some E2E scenarios live outside `wa-4vx.10.*` (for example setup safety, new IPC lanes, and UX overlays):
- `wa-nu4.3.3.10` (wa setup idempotency)
- `wa-4vx.2.7.3` (status_update IPC lane)
- `wa-nu4.1.6.4` (pane reservations)
- `wa-0go.7` (natural-language event descriptions)
- `wa-5em.9` (rollback visualization tests/E2E)
- `wa-dug.7` (environment detection + recommendations)
- `wa-tp4.8` (suggestion engine)
- `wa-nu4.4.3.5` (distributed E2E tests; non-default unless distributed feature enabled)
- `wa-9lh` (quick-fix suggestions E2E)
- `wa-am5` (dry-run mode E2E)
- `wa-jl5` (notification webhook delivery E2E)
- `wa-ugg` (timeline correlation E2E)
- `wa-p3i` (watch-and-notify mode E2E)

Suite completeness means the registry accounts for these cases too.
Implementation options (either is acceptable; pick the simplest):
- unify everything under `./scripts/e2e_test.sh` as runnable `--case` entries, OR
- allow registry entries to delegate to another script (adapter entry) while still producing the same artifacts contract.

## Change discipline
- Adding a new E2E case requires updating the registry.
- CI should catch:
  - “case exists but not in registry”
  - “case is in registry but not included in default suite” (unless explicitly tagged as non-default with justification)

## Registry
- This bead *is* the registry. Update the case list here when new E2E cases are added.

## Logging
- Follow the standard E2E harness logging contract (`wa-4vx.10.6`) and runner (`wa-4vx.10.11`).
- Emit per-step start/end markers with timestamps + durations.
- Include case name and pane ids in every log line.
- Redact secrets (device codes, tokens) in logs and artifacts.

## Acceptance Criteria
- A developer can run `./scripts/e2e_test.sh --all` and trust it covers the full default suite.
- The suite remains usable even as cases evolve across phases.
- `wa-nu4.3.3.10`, `wa-4vx.2.7.3`, `wa-nu4.1.6.4`, `wa-0go.7`, `wa-5em.9`, `wa-dug.7`, and `wa-tp4.8` are represented in the case registry (directly or via adapter), with stable artifacts.


## Testing
- Registry correctness tests:
  - Add a check that enumerates available E2E cases and fails if any are missing from the registry.
  - Add a check that fails if the registry references a non-existent case.

- `--all` behavior tests:
  - Include a small dummy registry in test mode to verify:
    - ordering and selection flags
    - summary format
    - per-case artifact dir naming is stable

 {"id":"wa-4vx.10.21","title":"E2E script: wa stop shuts down watcher (lock released, restart works)","description":"# Task: E2E script — wa stop

## Goal
Validate the user-facing shutdown path:
- wa watch is running
- wa stop stops it gracefully
- the lock is released
- restart works

This is a key UX reliability story for real users.

## Scenario
- Start mux server and spawn a dummy pane that prints a unique token.
- Start wa watch in workspace A.
- Wait until the token is persisted (wa robot search).
- Run wa stop --workspace A.

Assertions
- wa stop returns success.
- wa watch process exits.
- workspace lock is released.
- Starting wa watch again in workspace A succeeds.

## Artifacts
- wa watch logs
- wa stop logs/output
- effective config

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`), including prerequisites and default inclusion status.
- Ensure `./scripts/e2e_test.sh --all` includes this case (or mark non-default with justification).

## Logging
- Follow the standard E2E harness logging contract (`wa-4vx.10.6`) and runner (`wa-4vx.10.11`).
- Emit per-step start/end markers with timestamps + durations.
- Include case name and pane ids in every log line.
- Redact secrets (device codes, tokens) in logs and artifacts.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Test is deterministic.
- Failures clearly indicate whether the bug is in:
  - lock metadata
  - signal handling
  - shutdown flushing


## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - Add a negative case: `wa stop` when no watcher is running must return a clear, non-destructive message (and still exit 0/1 consistently as defined).
  - Assert that lock release is proven by attempting a second start (not by checking files alone).

- Artifact validation:
  - Include watcher PID/lock metadata evidence in artifacts so failures are actionable.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.10.22","title":"E2E script: wa doctor (healthy/broken) with verbose logs + artifacts","description":"# Task: E2E script — wa doctor

## Goal
Prove `wa doctor` works end-to-end as a **deterministic** health-check surface with **excellent logs/artifacts**.

Unit tests can validate individual checks, but this E2E case ensures the full command (arg parsing → config resolution → checks → output) behaves correctly and remains user-friendly.

## Scenario(s)

### Scenario A: Healthy workspace
- Create a fresh temp workspace (isolated DB/log dirs).
- Ensure stubbed dependencies report “healthy”:
  - wezterm CLI available + returns a plausible `wezterm cli list` output
  - DB opens and schema version matches
- Run:
  - `wa doctor --format json`

### Scenario B: Broken workspace (actionable failures)
Run doctor against controlled failures, one at a time:
- Missing wezterm CLI.
- Workspace not writable.
- DB cannot be opened / schema mismatch.

## Assertions
- Exit codes are correct and stable:
  - healthy → `0`
  - broken → non-zero
- JSON output is machine-parseable and contains:
  - a concise overall status
  - per-check status + actionable remediation hints
- No secrets appear in:
  - stdout JSON
  - stderr logs
  - artifact files

## Artifacts / logging contract
- Capture and store per-scenario artifacts in the standard E2E harness layout:
  - stdout (raw)
  - stderr (raw)
  - resolved workspace paths
  - any generated diagnostic files
  - timing summary (start/end + durations per step)

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`), including prerequisites and default inclusion status.
- Ensure `./scripts/e2e_test.sh --all` includes this case (or mark non-default with justification).

## Acceptance Criteria
- Script is deterministic and can run in CI.
- When it fails, the artifact bundle is sufficient to debug quickly.

## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - Ensure each “broken workspace” scenario fails for the intended reason (not cascading unrelated failures).
  - Add assertions that the remediation hints include stable `code` values so automation can key off them.

 {"id":"wa-bnm","title":"[EPIC] Actionable Quick-Fix Suggestions for Events and Errors","description":"# [EPIC] Actionable Quick-Fix Suggestions

## Mission
Every detected event and every error includes an **explicit, copy-pasteable command** to fix or handle it. No ambiguity about what to do next.

## Why This Matters
When something happens—an event is detected, an error occurs—users face a "now what?" moment:
- "Which command handles this event?"
- "How do I fix this error?"
- "What are my options?"

Quick-fix suggestions eliminate this friction:
- Copy-paste the suggested command
- See alternative approaches
- Learn the correct workflow

## How It Works

### For Events
```bash
$ wa events --unhandled

Unhandled Events (2):

1. codex.usage_limit_reached (Pane 9, 2 min ago)
   Codex hit its usage limit and needs account rotation.
   
   Quick fix: wa workflow run handle_usage_limits --pane 9
   Preview:   wa workflow run handle_usage_limits --pane 9 --dry-run
   Manual:    Exit Codex (Ctrl-C), log in with different account

2. session.compaction (Pane 3, 5 min ago)
   Claude Code compacted context and may have lost project state.
   
   Quick fix: wa workflow run handle_compaction --pane 3
   Preview:   wa workflow run handle_compaction --pane 3 --dry-run
   Manual:    Ask Claude to re-read AGENTS.md and key project files
```

### For Errors
```bash
$ wa watch
Error: WezTerm CLI not found

Quick fix: brew install wezterm  # macOS
           apt install wezterm   # Ubuntu
           See: https://wezfurlong.org/wezterm/install

Then retry: wa watch
```

```bash
$ wa send --pane 99 "hello"
Error: Pane 99 not found

Available panes: 1, 3, 7, 9
Did you mean:   wa send --pane 9 "hello"  (closest match)
List all:       wa status
```

### For Policy Denials
```bash
$ wa send --pane 3 "continue"
Error: Send denied - AltScreen active

Quick fix: Exit AltScreen (close vim/less/etc.) and retry
Why:       wa why denied --pane 3
```

## Implementation Components

### 1. Event Suggestion Templates
Each rule definition includes `suggested_action`:
```rust
RuleDef {
    id: "codex.usage_limit_reached",
    suggested_workflow: Some("handle_usage_limits"),
    manual_fix: "Exit Codex (Ctrl-C), log in with different account",
    // ...
}
```

### 2. Error Remediation System
Error types carry remediation hints:
```rust
enum WaError {
    WezTermNotFound {
        remediation: vec![
            Remediation::Command("brew install wezterm"),
            Remediation::Url("https://wezfurlong.org/wezterm/install"),
        ]
    },
    // ...
}
```

### 3. Context-Aware Suggestions
Use current state to generate relevant commands:
- Typo detection ("Did you mean pane 9?")
- Available resources ("Available panes: 1, 3, 7, 9")
- Environment-specific hints (macOS vs Linux)

## Design Principles
1. **Always show the command**: Not "run the workflow" but `wa workflow run X`
2. **Copy-friendly**: Commands can be copy-pasted directly
3. **Show alternatives**: Quick fix, preview, manual
4. **Progressive detail**: Brief first, expand on request

## Testing
- Unit tests: Each rule has valid suggestion templates
- Integration tests: Suggestions are generated for known scenarios
- UX tests: Suggestions are understandable and accurate

## Success Criteria
- All unhandled events include suggested commands
- All errors include "Quick fix:" with specific steps
- Suggestions are context-aware (show available panes, etc.)
- Users can copy-paste suggestions directly

## Acceptance Criteria
- Actionable suggestions appear for common error and event types.
- Suggestions are context-aware, redacted, and non-spammy.
- Suggested actions include stable IDs and references to fixes.
- All child tasks are complete and wa-bnm.4 tests pass.

 {"id":"wa-bnm.1","title":"Event suggestion templates: add suggested_action to all rule definitions","description":"
# Event Suggestion Templates

## Purpose
Extend rule definitions to include actionable suggestions for each detected event.

## Schema Extension
```rust
pub struct RuleDef {
    pub id: RuleId,
    pub pattern: PatternSpec,
    pub severity: Severity,
    // New fields:
    pub suggested_workflow: Option<String>,
    pub manual_fix: Option<String>,
    pub preview_command: Option<String>,
    pub learn_more_url: Option<String>,
}
```

## Example Rules
```toml
[[rules]]
id = "codex.usage_limit_reached"
suggested_workflow = "handle_usage_limits"
manual_fix = "Exit Codex (Ctrl-C), log in with different account"
preview_command = "wa workflow run handle_usage_limits --pane {pane} --dry-run"

[[rules]]
id = "session.compaction"
suggested_workflow = "handle_compaction"
manual_fix = "Ask the agent to re-read AGENTS.md and key project files"
preview_command = "wa workflow run handle_compaction --pane {pane} --dry-run"
```

## Command Template Interpolation
Support variables:
- {pane}: Pane ID where event detected
- {event_id}: Event ID for reference
- {agent}: Detected agent type

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All existing rules have suggestion fields
- [ ] Template interpolation works correctly
- [ ] Suggestions are actionable and accurate
- [ ] Manual fix provides human-friendly alternative

## Testing

 {"id":"wa-bnm.3","title":"Context-aware suggestions: typo detection, fuzzy matching, available resources","description":"
# Context-Aware Suggestions

## Purpose
Use current system state to provide smart, contextual suggestions in error messages.

## Features

### 1. Typo Detection
When user specifies non-existent resource, suggest closest match:
```
$ wa send --pane 99 "hello"
Error: Pane 99 not found

Did you mean: wa send --pane 9 "hello"  (closest match)
Available:    1, 3, 7, 9
```

Uses Levenshtein distance for string matching.

### 2. Available Resources
Always show what's available when something is not found:
```
Available panes: 1 (codex), 3 (claude), 7 (local)
Available workflows: handle_compaction, handle_usage_limits
Available rules: codex.*, claude.*, core.*
```

### 3. Environment-Aware Commands
Detect environment and tailor suggestions:
- macOS: homebrew commands
- Linux: apt/dnf/pacman commands
- Container: different paths

### 4. Recent State Hints
```
$ wa send --pane 3 "continue"
Error: Pane 3 is in AltScreen (vim)

Hint: Pane 3 entered AltScreen 2 minutes ago
Wait for: vim to exit, or use a different pane
```

## Implementation
```rust
pub struct SuggestionContext {
    pub available_panes: Vec<PaneInfo>,
    pub available_workflows: Vec<String>,
    pub platform: Platform,
    pub recent_state: Vec<StateChange>,
}

pub fn suggest_closest<T>(input: &str, candidates: &[T]) -> Option<T>;
pub fn format_available(items: &[T]) -> String;
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Typo suggestions for pane IDs
- [ ] Available resources shown on not-found errors
- [ ] Platform-specific commands detected automatically
- [ ] Recent state provides temporal context

## Testing
- Unit tests for template rendering and suggested_action interpolation.
- Integration tests: fixture errors/events produce actionable suggestions.
- E2E: extend an events/error scenario to assert suggestions and rule-id logs.

# Quick-Fix Testing Suite

## Purpose
Ensure all suggestions are accurate, copy-pasteable, and helpful.

## Test Categories

### 1. Template Interpolation Tests
- {pane} substitution works
- {event_id} substitution works
- Invalid templates fail gracefully

### 2. Error Remediation Coverage
- Every WaError variant has remediation

- Commands are syntactically valid

### 3. UX Validation Tests
- Commands can be copy-pasted (no smart quotes, etc.)
- Commands are actually runnable
- Suggestions match actual system state

### 4. Context-Aware Tests
- Typo detection finds correct suggestions
- Available resources are accurate
- Platform detection is correct

### 5. Snapshot Tests
- Error message format stability
- Suggestion format stability

## Test Fixtures
- Error scenarios with known remediations
- Event scenarios with known suggestions
- Platform-specific command sets

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] 100% of error types have remediation tests
- [ ] 100% of rule types have suggestion tests
- [ ] Snapshot tests for output stability
- [ ] Copy-paste validation (no encoding issues)

## Testing
- Unit tests for template rendering and suggested_action interpolation.
- Integration tests: fixture errors/events produce actionable suggestions.
- E2E: extend an events/error scenario to assert suggestions and rule-id logs.

 {"id":"wa-c7o","title":"[EPIC] Phase 2: Workflow Engine & Automation","description":"# Phase 2: Workflow Engine & Automation

## Overview
This epic builds the workflow automation system that responds to detected patterns. The workflow engine is what makes wa "active" rather than just a passive monitor - it can take corrective actions when agents hit limits, experience compaction, need authentication, etc.

## Strategic Importance
The workflow system transforms wa from a "monitoring tool" into an "automation platform". This is where the real value emerges:
- Automatic context recovery after compaction
- Automatic account rotation on usage limits
- Automatic authentication renewal
- Foundation for more sophisticated multi-agent coordination

## Key Components

### 1. Workflow Engine Architecture
- Trait-based workflow definition: \`Workflow\` trait with name, description, handles, steps, execute_step
- Step results: Continue, Done, Retry, Abort, WaitFor
- WaitCondition: Pattern, PaneIdle, External
- Per-pane workflow locking (prevent conflicting workflows)
- Idempotent, recoverable, audited execution

### 2. Handle Usage Limits Workflow (Codex First)
Steps:
1. Exit current session gracefully (Ctrl-C twice for Codex)
2. Parse session info for resume (session ID, token usage)
3. Select next available account
4. Perform account authentication
5. Resume previous session
6. Send continue prompt

This is the most complex workflow and proves out the architecture.

### 3. Handle Compaction Workflow
Steps:
1. Wait for compaction to complete (stabilization window)
2. Send agent-specific context refresh prompt
   - Claude Code: "Reread AGENTS.md so it's still fresh in your mind."
   - Codex: "Please re-read AGENTS.md and any key project context files."
   - Gemini: "Please re-examine AGENTS.md and project context."

### 4. Browser Automation Skeleton
- Playwright integration structure
- Browser profile persistence
- OpenAI device auth flow (skeleton)
- Anthropic OAuth flow (skeleton)
- Google auth flow (skeleton)
- Semi-automated fallback (open browser for human when automation fails)

### 5. CAUT Integration
- Shell out to \`caut\` for usage tracking
- get_usage(service) - current usage for OpenAI/Anthropic/Google
- refresh_usage(service) - refresh from APIs
- Account selection logic based on percent_remaining

## Workflow Design Constraints
Every workflow must be:
- **Idempotent**: Re-running doesn't make a mess
- **Recoverable**: Can resume after a crash
- **Audited**: Every step recorded (what was observed, what was sent)
- **Guarded**: Each step validates state before action

## Workflow Execution Model
Each step follows: Observe → Decide → Act → Verify

Step can return:
- \`Continue\` - proceed to next step
- \`WaitFor(condition)\` - pause until condition met
- \`Retry(after)\` - retry same step after delay
- \`Abort(reason)\` - stop workflow with error
- \`Done(result)\` - workflow completed successfully

## Data-Driven Workflow Descriptors
In addition to compiled Rust workflows, support YAML/TOML descriptors for simple prompt injection sequences:

\`\`\`yaml
# ~/.config/wa/workflows/custom_compaction.yaml
name: custom_compaction_prompt
trigger:
  event: session.compaction
  agent: claude_code
steps:
  - wait_stable: 2s
  - send: |
      Please re-read AGENTS.md and the project's README.md.
  - wait_for: "❯"
\`\`\`

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Workflow engine can execute multi-step workflows
- [ ] handle_compaction workflow works end-to-end for Claude Code
- [ ] handle_usage_limits workflow works end-to-end for Codex (at least skeleton)
- [ ] Browser automation structure is in place (can be extended in Phase 3)
- [ ] CAUT integration returns account usage data
- [ ] Workflows are audited and recoverable

## Dependencies
- Depends on Phase 1 (Foundation) - needs pattern engine, storage, watcher

## Technical Notes

### Why Codex First for Usage Limits?
- Codex has the clearest device auth flow
- Resume mechanism is well-documented
- Good test case for the complexity

### Semi-Automated Browser Auth
If Playwright can't complete auth (MFA, CAPTCHA, password):
1. Open non-headless browser
2. Request human to complete auth
3. Persist browser profile for future sessions
4. Notify when auth complete

This provides graceful degradation.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","assignee":"","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T08:47:54.895212760Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-18T08:55:07.794108825Z","closed_at":"2026-01-18T08:55:07.794108825Z","close_reason":"Duplicate of wa-nu4.1 (Phase 2: Workflows). wa-nu4.1 has correct dependency chain.
